coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent label free probe for Chromium (II) ions using carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorous
coredata.dc:creator: Omer, Khalid M.
Tofiq, Diary I.
Ghafoor, Dlizar D.
coredata.dc:description: Analytical probe for determination of Chromium (II) ions is almost rare in contrary to tremendous probes for chromium (III) and chromium (VI). Here, highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with nitrogen and phosphorous were prepared solvothermally. Size, morphology, surface structure, chemical composition, and optical properties of the CQDs were characterized. The aqueous solution of CQDs showed high selectivity towards Cr2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity quenched with Cr2+ ions. The energy level calculations showed that the quenching mechanism is attributed to the transfer of electron from HOMO orbitals of Cr2+ to the photo-hole formed in the valence band of CQDs, as the energy levels’ alignment rules out transition of photoexcited electron from the CQDs’ LUMO to the LUMO of Cr2+. Due to highly stability of the CQDs under rigorous conditions, such as; low and high pHs, high ionic strength, photostability, and thermal stability, The CQDs will be good candidate nanomaterials for rugged, robust, selective, and sensitive analytical probe. Lower limit of detection as small as 0.1 μM was calculated (with S/N = 3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chromium (II)
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence probe
Quenching

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research for supporting this publication.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A scheme describing the preparation of the CQDs.
(A) TEM images of CQDs, (B) histogram counting 200 nanoparticles. (C) XRD spectra of the CQDs, (D) FTIR of the CQDs.

High resolution XPS of the CQDs on silicone foil. (A) Survey scan shows all the elements carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorous and silicone peaks. Deconvoluted spectra of (B) C 1 s, (C) O 1 s, (D) N 1 s, (E) P 2p. CasaXPS software was used for fitting the peaks.
(A) Absorption, excitation, and photoluminescence spectra of the CQDs in an aqueous solution. (B) Tauc plot to extract the energy gap between the valence band and conduction band.
Cyclic voltammetry of CQDs on the glassy carbon electrode (2 mm diameter), Ag/AgCl and carbon rod as a reference and counter electrode respectively.

(A) Digital photograph showing common metal ions with the CQDs under 365 nm UV light in the dark, (B) UV–Vis spectra for free CQDs, free Cr2+ ions, and CQDs:Cr2+ (C) Schematic diagram shows energy levels of CQDs with Cr2+ ions and how hole transfer from CQDs to the Cr2+.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of free CQDs with different concentration of Cr2+ ions. (B) Calibration graph for series Cr2+ concentrations, (C) effect of some common metal ions on the fluorescence of CQDs.
Recovery of Cr2+ ions in tap water using CQDs as a label-free probe (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent label free probe for Chromium (II) ions using carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorous

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Sulaimani-Kirkuk Main Road, 46002 Sulaimani, Kurdistan, Iraq

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Sulaimani-Kirkuk Main Road, 46002 Sulaimani, Kurdistan, Iraq.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Analytical probe for determination of Chromium (II) ions is almost rare in contrary to tremendous probes for chromium (III) and chromium (VI). Here, highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doped with nitrogen and phosphorous were prepared solvothermally. Size, morphology, surface structure, chemical composition, and optical properties of the CQDs were characterized. The aqueous solution of CQDs showed high selectivity towards Cr2+ ions and the fluorescence intensity quenched with Cr2+ ions. The energy level calculations showed that the quenching mechanism is attributed to the transfer of electron from HOMO orbitals of Cr2+ to the photo-hole formed in the valence band of CQDs, as the energy levels’ alignment rules out transition of photoexcited electron from the CQDs’ LUMO to the LUMO of Cr2+. Due to highly stability of the CQDs under rigorous conditions, such as; low and high pHs, high ionic strength, photostability, and thermal stability, The CQDs will be good candidate nanomaterials for rugged, robust, selective, and sensitive analytical probe. Lower limit of detection as small as 0.1 μM was calculated (with S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots, CQDs, are nano-sized carbon materials, have diameters below 10 nm and have properties between individual atoms and larger particles. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become interesting and promising nanomaterials due to their unique properties, such as; high fluorescence, photo-stability, biocompatibility, and relatively facile preparation [1–3]. for that, CQDs have been used for many applications, like; sensors [4–7], bio-imaging [8], photocatalysis [9,10], electrocatalysis [11,12]. and electrogenerated chemiluminescence [13–17].

It is well known in analytical chemistry that selectivity, sensitivity, and stability are figures of merits that are sought for quantitative determination of an analyte in a sample. One should consider those criteria in designing a new analytical probe. CQDs are promising candidate to achieve the mentioned criteria as they offer high fluorescence quantum yields which necessary for sensitive analytical assay. Moreover, most CQDs contain surface functional groups which make them selective towards a particular species [18–21]. Additionally, some CQDs are pH insensitive, thermo and photostable that lead to a robust and rugged probe [22].

Chromium divalent is not an abundant species of chromium in nature as other forms, such as chromium (III) and chromium (VI), due to less stability of Cr2+, however, chromium divalent has been seen in earth Mantle in some Olivines under high pressure and elevated temprature [23]. In redox reactions, Cr2+ is relatively a strong redcuing agent (E1/2 = −0.91 V vs NHE). Thus, designing a fluorescence probe is of great interest from analytical and material chemistry perspective.

In this paper, we present fabrication of highly fluorescent blue emitter of nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped CQDs from four components via hydrothermal procedure. Fig. 1 shows the preparation scheme of the CQDs. The blue CQD emitters were irresponsive to most of metal ions and anions, while they quenched after addition of Cr2+ ions. Label-free fluorescence method is proposed for determination of Cr2+ ions with high sensitivity and selectivity. Up to our knowledge, this is the first report on quantitative determination of Cr2+ ions using carbon nanodots. Quenching mechanism based on absorbance measurements and energy levels was postulated.

All chemicals used were of analytical grades. Trimesic acid was purchased from sigma (98%, Sigma-Aldrich). Urea (99.9%, for analysis ACS) was purchased from Acros Organic. 85% o-phosphoric acid was obtained from Fisher. Polyethylenimine, branched was obtained from sigma (Mw ~800, Sigma Aldrich). Stock solution of Cr2+ ions from Cr(CH3COO)2 dimer monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). All other metal ions were obtained from sigma-aldrich as nitrate or sulfate salts.

UV–Vis spectra were collected on Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). FTIR spectra were recorded on Nicolet iS5 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) with KBr pellets. Photoluminescence spectra were recorded on Fluorolog 3 Fluorimeter (Horiba Scientific, USA). Cyclic voltammograms were recorded on CHI660 (CHI, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to observe the morphology and size of CQDs, which was carried out on a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan). The TEM samples were prepared by drying a droplet of dilute solution of the CQDs on a carbon supported copper grid. The surface and core chemical composition of the CQDs were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which was measured with Krato XPS spectrometer (Krato, UK) using a monochromatic Al Kα line at 1486.6 eV. Binding energies were calibrated with respect to the C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV. Peak fit analysis was performed using the CasaXPS.

In a typical experiment, 0.5 gm trimesic acid, 0.5 gm urea, 0.5 mL polyethylene diamine branched, and 10 mL concentrated H3PO4 were mixed and sonicated for 10 min. The solution was heated at 180 °C in a stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave for 15 h (Fig. 1). The resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for 30 min, followed by dialysis for 10 h using 1000 MWCO dialysis tube against deionized water.

In order to evaluate the PL quantum yield, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a reference [24]. by comparison the quantum yield can be calculated as follow:
Φ
T
=
Φ
R
×
(
F
T
/
F
R
)
×
(
η
T
/ η
R
)
2

ΦT and ΦR Is the quantum yields of the test and reference. FT and FR are the integrated fluorescence intensity of both the CQDs and the reference.η refers to the refractive index (1.33 for water and a 0.1 mol/L H2SO4). In order to minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance values should never exceed 0.1.

The electrochemical measurements of CQDs were investigated by cyclic voltammetry. Conventional three electrode setup was used, graphite rod and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrodes. Glassy carbon electrode modified with the CQDs was used as a working electrode, and 0.1 M KCl as a supporting electrolyte. The bare glassy carbon was polished with slurries of alumina and silica to create a mirror polish, then dissolving the traces of silica in a 1 M KOH for 30 s. It was then sonicated for 3 min in acetone, ethanol, and H2O for 5 min in each. The shiny surface of electrode was checked under the optical microscope to assure the mirror polish of the electrode. Then, in a small vial, 100 μL of the CQDs was mixed with 100 μL Nafion 0.05% and the mixture mixed thoroughly for three minutes in a vortex. This solution then casted on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode using microsyringe, and left to dry at room temperature.

All metal ion solutions were prepared in 0.05 M HNO3, then diluted with deionized water. A typical procedure for determination of Cr2+ ions, 1 mL CQDs was mixed with different concentrations of Cr2+ ions to make serial solutions. The fluorescence emission was recorded after 2 min. The pH of the measurements of each solution were adjusted between 2.0 and 3.0 with HCl or NaOH.

In order to examine the size of the CQDS, TEM image was recorded as shown in Fig. 2. The size and form of nanoparticles were spherical and around 2.0–3.5 ± 0.5 nm. X-ray diffraction spectra showed one broad peak around 27°, which indicates to graphitic structure of the CQDs [25,26].

The chemical structure of the surface CQDs was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 2D shows the main functional group on the surface of the CQDs, broad peak around 3500 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching mode of O-H, and at 2900 cm−1 to the C-H stretching. 1640 cm−1 assigns for C=C of the graphene [27]. further confirmation of the surface chemistry and the chemical composition of the prepared CQDs was conducted using high resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3A-D displays the survey and deconvoluted spectra of each identified elements. The survey spectra (Fig. 3A) shows four major peaks corresponding to O 1 s (∼532 eV), C 1 s (∼ 283 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and P 2p (133 eV).

C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3B) shows four peaks at 282.9, 283.3, 284.5, corresponding to graphitic sp2 C=C, C-O/C-OH, C=O, respectively [28]. O 1 s spectrum shows three peaks centered at 529.1, 530.9, and 531.1 eV corresponding to C-O, C=O, and O-H, respectively [29]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two peaks, at 398.1 eV which corresponds to C-N-C nitrogen atom, and the second one 400.1 eV corresponds to N-H eV [30,31]. Fig. 3E shows the resolved spectra of P 2p, wich the best fit just showed one peak at 133.1 eV corresponding to C-P [32–34]. which indicates that the C-dots are doped with phosphorous atoms. Appearance of nitrogen and phosphorous peaks in the XPS indicate that the prepared CDs are phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped. The obtained results here are in a good agreement with the reported in literature, as CQDs that were solvothermaly prepared using phosphoric acid and using nitrogen containing substrate show nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped with carbon dots [10].

Optical properties of the CQDs were examined using UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometry and steady state fluorescence. Fig. 4A shows the absorption spectra display a typical 284 nm absorption peak assigns for π to π * transition of graphene sheets. One can see another broad absorption peak between 340 and 370 nm that can be attributed to functional groups on the surface of CQDs [34].

photoluminescence and excitation spectra for CQDs in the aqueous solution were taken as shown in Fig. 4A. The maximum excitation spectra peaked around 340 nm, and emission spectra at 440 nm. The inset of Fig. 4A shows the digital photograph of the CQDs in the aqueous solution under well-lit room and under UV excitation. In order to calculate the energy gap between the conduction band and valance of the CQDs, Tauc plot were constructed as shown in Fig. 4B. Based on the Tauc plot, 3.2 eV is the energy gap (Eg or band gap) of the CQDs.

To be able to find the energy levels, conduction and valence bands, cyclic voltammetry of the CQDs were performed in a conventional three-electrode system, as shown in Fig. 5. During the negative sweep, one broad peak recorded at 0.66 V vs Ag/AgCl (−0.87 V vs NHE). One can calculate the lowest occupied molecular orbital energy (ELUMO or conduction band) as follow:
E
LUMO
= – 4.5 –
( Ered )
here
Eredis
the
reduction
peak
potential
of
CQDs .
= − 4.5 –
(
0.87
)
= – 3.63
eV
E
HOMO
= – Eg –
E
LUMO
= – 6.83
eV

In order to make an analytical probe, one should examine pH effect, ionic strength effect, photon and thermal stability. Fig. 6A shows that the PL intensity of CQDs are stable under low pHs and higher pHs. Same trend was observed for lower ionic strength, 0.01 M NaCl, to higher ionic strength such as 2.00 M NaCl. Long-term photostability test was also examined as shown in Fig. 6B via irradiation of CQDs solution under full spectrum Xenon light (100 mW/cm2). The fluorescence intensity showed negligible decrease less than 5% after 30 h of irradiation. However, heat effect showed decrease in fluorescence up to 20% loss of emission after heating up to 150 °C.

Photoluminescence quantum yield was calculated to be 0.41 based on comparison method taking quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference. High PL quantum yield with stability under rigorous conditions such as lower and higher pHs, high ionic strength, photo and thermos stability. All those properties will make the prepared CQDs a highly candidate nanomaterial for analytical application.

The fluorescence of CQDs was almost unaffected by addition most of the metal ions ( Fig. 7A), while it quenches after addition of chromium (II) ions. The tested metal ions were: Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Bi3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, and Pb2+. The anions were SO42-, NO3 -, NO2 -, and ClO-.

Cr2+ ion is not abundant as Cr3+ and Cr6+. However, chromium (II) acetate has been used as a reducing agent. Fig. 7B shows the absorbance of free CQDs, Cr2+ alone, and CQDs combined with Cr2+ ions. One can notice the absorbance of CQDs:Cr2+ is not merely a combination of two absorbance rather than is a new spectrum. Absorbance spectra of other common metal ions alone and with CQDs were recorded as shown in Fig. S2. The quenching mechanisms of carbon dots have been attributed to some reasons, common reasons are; photoelectron transfer (PET), chemical reactions. In PET, which majority of carbon dots’ quenching happen via this mechanism, attribute the quenching to the non-radiative relaxation of photoexcited electron [35,36]. this route depends on the higher energy levels of the LUMO of CQDs (conduction band, CB) comparing to the LUMO of the metal ions to make photoelectron transfer energetically favorable. Based on our electrochemical and absorbance measurement, ELUMO of the CQDs is −3.63 eV, while for Cr2+ is −3.59 eV (ECr2+/Cr0 is −0.91 V vs NHE)[37]. Hence it rules out the quenching to be from PET from Cr2+ to CQDs. While, EHOMO of Cr2+ is −6.00 eV, which is higher energy than valence band energy of the CQDs, Evb = –6.83 eV. Thus, the electrons in the HOMO of Cr2+ will transfer to photo-hole formed in the CQDs quenching the fluorescence (Fig. 7C)

On the other hand, the absorbance spectra confirm that there is a reaction or chelation between the CQDs’ functional groups with Cr2+ ions (Fig. 7B). The chelation will facilitate the quenching mechanism. Fig. S3 shows the solution of highly concentrated Cr2+ ions (0.01 M) with CQDs after two weeks, which forms a purple color precipitate. This might be an indicator to the slow formation of Cr3+ complexes, as amine rich group can react with Cr2+ to form a purple complex [38].

On noticed point that is worthy to mention is Cr2+ can quench the fluorescence of CQDs within seconds after the addition, however, metal ions like Fe2+ and Co2+ can partially quench with 5–6 h. There reason can be attributed to that both Fe2+ and Co2+ are also reducing agent and give electron to the valence band of the CQDs, as one can look at the thermodynamic reduction potentials of both metal ions:
Fe2+ + 2e → Fe(s) E° = – 0.41 V vs NHE
Co2+ + 2e → Co(s) E° = – 0.28 V vs NHE

However, the slow quenching kinetics of Fe2+ and Co2+ might be due to non-chelation process between CQDs and the metal ions, which does not facilitate the electron transfer process, and weak reducing property of Fe2+ and Co2+ comparing to Cr2+.

Based on the fluorescence quenching via addition of Cr2+, a fluorescent probe was employed for a quantitative determination of Cr2+ ions. Different concentrations of Cr2+ ions were mixed with a fixed proportion of CQDs and their emission intensity were recorded. The intensity of CQDs decreases with increasing the concentration of Cr2+ ( Fig. 8A). plot of Fo/F (where Fo fluorescence intensity in the absence of Cr2+ ion, F fluorescence intensity in the presence of Cr2+ ions) versus concentration of Cr2+ ions gives a linear relationship in the range 0.5–1.3 μM. The detection limit calculated based on signal to noise ration = 3, was 0.1 μM. Assessing the selectivity is highly important for making a real-time analytical probe. For this, different common metal ions and anions were testes, samples of the ions were shown in Figure 8 C. 100 μM from each ion was chosen and mixed with a fixed portion of CQDs. The tested ions were Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Al3+, Bi3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, and Cl-. Experimental results showed that the CQDs was very selective to Cr2+ ions, which assures the potential application of CQDs to be used for real-time detection of Cr2+ ions.

To examine using the CQDs as a label fee probe for an application, tap water was used using spike standard addition as a validity test. The real sample of Cr2+ ions is very rare, we just use a tap water to confirm the validity of our proposed probe with the presence of a matrix effect. The sample was spiked with 0.70, 1.00, and 1.30 μM of Cr2+ ions. As shown in Table 1, the spike recoveries were 99%, 97%, and 102%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD%) 1.02, 1.05, and 0.96 confirming that the probe has a high accuracy and precision.

Blue emitters of nitrogen and phosphorous doped CQDs have been prepared via solvothermal method using four components. Aqueous solution of CQDs were highly stable under rigorous conditions, such as high and low pHs, high ionic strength, and long-term full spectrum irradiation of UV–visible light which make the proposed probe rugged and robust. The prepared CQDs were highly selective for interaction with Cr2+ ions accompanying quenching of their fluorescence emission that make them a suitable nanomaterial for fluorescence probe. Cr2+ ions could be determined down to 0.2 μM.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research for supporting this publication.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.100.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Kinetics of dissociation of the chromium(II) acetate dimer
